comment_text.sn5.1:1.1: This chapter naturally follows the previous, as it features Māra challenging various bhikkhunīs. Some of these verses are also included in the Therīgāthā, without the prose narrative. The commentarial backgrounds for the nuns are available in many sources, including Malalasekera’s Dictionary of Pali proper Names, and I will not repeat them here.
comment_text.sn5.1:1.3: Her name means “of Āḷavī”. This exchange (lacking Āḷavikā’s first verse) is echoed in Thig 3.7, where the author is named Selā. The commentary confirms that she is the same person, Selā of Āḷavī.
comment_text.sn5.1:1.4: Normally one goes “for the day’s meditation” (divāvihārāya), whereas here, uniquely, the text has “seeking seclusion” (vivekatthinī). In line with this, the next sentence has Māra wanting her to “fall away from seclusion”, whereas normally it is “fall away from immersion”. Seclusion also occurs in Māra’s challenge. | The Dark Forest (andhavana) was a thick grove south of Sāvatthī often visited by monks and nuns for meditation. The commentary says the name means “Blind Man’s Forest”, giving an improbable origin story. But compare Sanskrit andhakūpa, “dark well” or “blind well”, a well covered over with a lid or overgrowth; also the name of a hell.
comment_text.sn5.1:5.1: This image is often found independently, and along with other similes at MN 22:3.17 and AN 5.76:15.4. The verse as a whole in found thrice in Therīgāthā: the verses of Selā (Thig 3.7:2.1), Khemā (Thig 6.3:3.1), and Uppalavaṇṇā (Thig 11.1:11.1).
comment_text.sn5.1:5.2: Khandāsaṁ resolves to khandā esaṁ.

root_text.sn5.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn5.1:1.3: Atha kho āḷavikā bhikkhunī pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn5.1:1.4: Sāvatthiyaṁ piṇḍāya caritvā pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkantā yena andhavanaṁ tenupasaṅkami vivekatthinī.
root_text.sn5.1:1.5: Atha kho māro pāpimā āḷavikāya bhikkhuniyā bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo vivekamhā cāvetukāmo yena āḷavikā bhikkhunī tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āḷavikaṁ bhikkhuniṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.1:3.2: “ko nu khvāyaṁ manusso vā amanusso vā gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti?
root_text.sn5.1:3.4: “māro kho ayaṁ pāpimā mama bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo vivekamhā cāvetukāmo gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti.
root_text.sn5.1:3.5: Atha kho āḷavikā bhikkhunī “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāhi paccabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.1:6.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ āḷavikā bhikkhunī”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn5.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn5.1:1.3: Then the nun Āḷavikā robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
translation_text.sn5.1:1.4: She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest seeking seclusion.
translation_text.sn5.1:1.5: Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Āḷavikā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from seclusion, went up to her and addressed her in verse:
translation_text.sn5.1:2.2: so what will seclusion do for you?
translation_text.sn5.1:3.2: “Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?”
translation_text.sn5.1:3.4: “This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from seclusion!”
translation_text.sn5.1:3.5: Then Āḷavikā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse:
translation_text.sn5.1:4.1: “There is an escape in the world,
translation_text.sn5.1:4.2: and I’ve personally experienced it with wisdom.
translation_text.sn5.1:4.3: O Wicked One, kinsman of the negligent,
translation_text.sn5.1:5.1: Sensual pleasures are like swords and spears;
translation_text.sn5.1:5.2: the aggregates are their chopping block.
translation_text.sn5.1:5.4: is now no delight for me.”
translation_text.sn5.1:6.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Āḷavikā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.
